title: Hearing - International Fuels Tax Agreement (IFTA) - 202512022H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-27004

CPA HEARING NO. 121,375

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:  January 1, 2021 THROUGH June 30, 2023


I. F. T. A./RDT

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


JANICE CAHALANE

Representing Respondent


INDIVIDUAL

Representing Petitioner


 

 


COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on January 6, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written. 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as written is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a liability.[2]

 

The total sum of the tax, penalty, and interest is due and payable 20 days after a comptroller’s decision becomes final.[3]  If such sum is not timely paid, an additional penalty of 10 percent of the taxes due will accrue.


SIGNED on this 12th day of December 2025
 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-25-27004                                                   

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 121,375

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) audited ************** (Petitioner) for fuel tax compliance under the International Fuel Tax Agreement (IFTA) and made an assessment that included tax, penalty, and accrued interest. Petitioner requested redetermination of the assessment, contending that its fuel statements were not used in the audit and that it would provide additional documentation to support adjustments. Petitioner also requested waivers of penalty and interest. Staff contends that Petitioner failed to show error in the audit. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends the assessment be upheld.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred this case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on August 29, 2025, issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. On September 17, 2025, ALJ Kimberly LaFlair issued an order setting the written submission hearing. Janice Cahalane represented Staff, and Petitioner was represented by its president, INDIVIDUAL. The contested case record closed October 28, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. Reasons for Decision

 

A.Evidence

 

Petitioner submitted additional argument and offered the following exhibits in the written submission hearing: [ENDNOTE 4]

 


	
	Summary of Truck Inventory from January 2021 to June 2023;
	
	
	IFTA Compliance Report Overview (2021-June 2023);
	
	
	Detailed Mileage Breakdown by Truck Number, State, Year, and Quarter (January 2021-June 2023);
	
	
	Comprehensive Analysis of Total Miles;
	
	
	Aggregated Mileage Data by State, Year, and Quarter (January 2021-June 2023);
	
	
	Complete Set of Bills of Lading for all Trips from January 2021 to June 2023;
	
	
	Trip Sheets;
	
	
	Fuel Reports from Fleet One;
	
	
	Comprehensive Fuel Purchase Reports; and
	
	
	Credit Card Transaction Records for Fuel Purchases.
	


 

Staff submitted the pleadings exchanged between the parties prior to referring the matter to SOAH and offered the following exhibits:


	
	Ninety-Day Letter;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Audit Results;
	
	
	Penalty and Interest Waiver Worksheet;
	
	
	Audit Package;
	
	
	Audit Documentation Report/Audit Plan; and
	
	
	Letter of Introduction.
	


 

The parties’ exhibits were admitted without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

There are no agreed adjustments.

 

C. Material Facts and Issues Presented

 

During the period at issue, Petitioner was an IFTA licensee based in CITY, Texas. Texas was Petitioner’s IFTA base jurisdiction. In August 2023, Staff notified Petitioner that it would be conducting an audit for the period of January 1, 2021, through June 30, 2023, and requested Petitioner’s mileage and fuel records for the audit period.

 

Petitioner provided the auditor with fuel records, fuel vendor statements, miles, daily register reports in Excel, IFTA return copies, lease agreements, and a truck list with 22 trucks. The auditor noted that the daily register reports appeared to be created by Petitioner, and that the miles records were incomplete and not adequate for auditing. Additionally, the fuel statements included approximately 10,000 more gallons than Petitioner reported. Because the mileage records were incomplete, the auditor estimated the audit, giving Petitioner credit for reported gallons and lowering the reported miles per gallon by 20%.

 

On June 13, 2024, Staff issued a Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner, assessing tax, 10% penalty, and accrued interest. Petitioner requested redetermination of the assessment, generally contending that its fuel statements were not used in the audit and that it would provide additional documentation not provided during the audit. Petitioner also requested waivers of penalty and interest. Staff did not agree to adjust the assessment and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

Petitioner provided additional argument and evidence in the written submission hearing that it contends substantiates its claims regarding IFTA-reported miles, trip miles, fuel purchases, tax calculations, and related discrepancies for the audit period. Staff reviewed Petitioner’s evidence and filed a response, stating that Petitioner’s exhibits were insufficient to support adjustments to the assessment because they consisted of unsupported summary spreadsheets and purported Bills of Lading.

 

D. Analysis and Recommendation

 

The IFTA Articles of Agreement (IFTA Art.) allow a licensee to report and pay motor fuel use taxes to a base jurisdiction for distribution to other member jurisdictions in which the licensee traveled and incurred motor fuel use tax liability. IFTA Art. I, R130.100.005. The base jurisdiction must enforce those duties and obligations within its jurisdiction and shall audit licensee records and issue assessments in accordance with the laws of the jurisdiction. IFTA Art. I, R140; Art. XII R1210.300.

 

The IFTA licensee must maintain records to substantiate its returns, and recordkeeping requirements shall be specified in the IFTA Procedures Manual. IFTA Art. VII, R700. A licensee must retain the records of its operations to which IFTA reporting requirements apply for a period of four years following the date the IFTA tax return for such operations was due or was filed, whichever is later, plus any period covered by waivers or jeopardy assessments. IFTA Procedures Manual P510. A licensee must preserve all fuel and distance records for the period covered by the quarterly tax returns for any periods under audit in accordance with the laws of the base jurisdiction. Id.

 

The records maintained by an IFTA licensee must be adequate to enable the base jurisdiction to verify the distances traveled and fuel purchased by the licensee for the period under audit and to evaluate the accuracy of the licensee’s distance and fuel accounting systems for its fleet. Id. P530. If a licensee fails to maintain IFTA required records, the base jurisdiction may determine the tax liability based on the best information available using factors such as prior experience of the licensee, similar operations, industry averages, or other pertinent information. IFTA Art. XII, R1210.100.015, R1210.200.005; IFTA Audit Manual A350.200.

 

The assessment made by a base jurisdiction under the procedure provided by IFTA is presumed to be correct and, in any case where the validity of the assessment is questioned, the burden is on the licensee to establish by a fair preponderance of evidence that the assessment is erroneous or excessive. IFTA Art. XII, R1210.300; IFTA Procedures Manual P520.

 

Staff’s evidence supports a conclusion that the audit was based on the best information available and conducted pursuant to IFTA procedures; therefore, it is presumed correct. Because Petitioner contends that the audit did not include its fuel statements, it must establish by a preponderance of evidence that the assessment is erroneous or excessive.

 

Here, the audit was estimated because the auditor deemed Petitioner’s records incomplete. Although Petitioner did not provide Staff with additional documentation during the hearings process to support adjustments, it did provide voluminous documentation as exhibits in the written submission hearing. Those documents included summaries of truck inventory, an IFTA compliance overview showing total trip miles compared to IFTA reported miles, mileage summaries by state and quarter, and bills of lading for trips during the audit period. Petitioner also provided a statement in support of relief from penalties and a summary of its exhibits.

 

Staff argues that Petitioner’s exhibits and the documentation provided during the audit were reviewed, considered, and found to be insufficient to support adjustments to the assessment. Petitioner’s summary spreadsheets, Staff argues, are not accompanied by explanations, source records, or indicia of completeness or reliability, and therefore insufficient to rebut the audit’s presumption of correctness as found in Comptroller’s Decision No. 102,734 (2012). As for the bills of lading, Staff contends that those documents serve as receipts for goods shipped and do not contain information such as truck origin, truck destination, routes, dates, truck assignment, or milage driven by the truck, as claimed by Petitioner.

 

The ALJ agrees that the documentation provided by Petitioner is insufficient to support adjustments to the assessment. As discussed above, IFTA has record keeping requirements that must be adequate to enable the verification of the distances traveled and fuel purchased for the period under audit and to evaluate the accuracy of the licensee’s distance and fuel accounting systems for its fleet. The ALJ finds that Petitioner failed to establish that the audit was erroneous, and the assessment should be upheld.

 

An IFTA base jurisdiction may assess the licensee a penalty of $50.00 or 10 percent of delinquent taxes, whichever is greater, for failing to file a tax return, filing a late tax return, [or] underpaying taxes due. IFTA Art. R1220.100. Penalty may be waived “for reasonable cause.” IFTA Art. XII, R1260.100. In this case, Petitioner requested penalty waiver and provided a statement as to why it believes penalty should be waived but provided no evidence regarding a reasonable cause that led to the amount of tax due. In light of the absence of any evidence, the ALJ concludes penalty should be upheld.

 

Petitioner also requested interest waiver. The IFTA base jurisdiction, for itself and on behalf of the other jurisdictions, assesses interest on all delinquent taxes due to each jurisdiction except taxes collected directly by other jurisdictions. See IFTA Art. XII, R1230. If a licensee can demonstrate a tax return was filed late because of misinformation given to the carrier by the base jurisdiction, the interest may be waived for the base jurisdiction if the jurisdiction’s statutes allow such a waiver. IFTA Art. XII, R1260. Here, Petitioner has not provided documentation of the existence of any of the factors meriting waiver of interest, and the ALJ concludes that Petitioner’s request for interest waiver should be denied.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	************** (Petitioner) was an IFTA licensee based in CITY, Texas, and Texas was Petitioner’s IFTA base jurisdiction.
	
	
	In August 2023, the Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) notified Petitioner that it would be conducting an audit for the period of January 1, 2021, through June 30, 2023, and requested Petitioner’s mileage and fuel records for the audit period.
	
	
	Petitioner provided the auditor with fuel records, fuel vendor statements, miles, daily register reports in Excel, IFTA return copies, lease agreements, and a truck list with 22 trucks.
	
	
	The auditor noted that the daily register reports appeared to be created by Petitioner, and that the miles records were incomplete and not adequate for auditing. The fuel statements included approximately 10,000 more gallons than Petitioner reported.
	
	
	The auditor estimated the audit, giving Petitioner credit for reported gallons and lowering the miles per gallon (MPG) by 20%.
	
	
	On June 13, 2024, Staff issued a Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner, assessing tax, 10% penalty, and accrued interest.
	
	
	Petitioner requested redetermination of the assessment.
	
	
	Staff did not agree to adjust the assessment and referred the matter to State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On August 29, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. The notice contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On September 17, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair issued an order setting the written submission hearing.
	
	
	The contested case record closed on October 28, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing in this matter, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notice of the hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	IFTA’s Articles of Agreement (IFTA Art.) allow a licensee to report and pay motor fuel use taxes to a base jurisdiction for distribution to other member jurisdictions in which the licensee traveled and incurred motor fuel use tax liability. IFTA Art. I, R130.100.005.
	
	
	The base jurisdiction must enforce those duties and obligations within its jurisdiction and shall audit licensee records and issue assessments in accordance with the laws of the jurisdiction. IFTA Art. I, R140 & Art. XII, R1210.300.
	
	
	The IFTA licensee must maintain records to substantiate its returns. IFTA Art. VII, R700.
	
	
	A licensee must retain the records of its operations to which IFTA reporting requirements apply for a period of four years following the date the IFTA tax return for such operations was due or was filed, whichever is later, plus any period covered by waivers or jeopardy assessments. IFTA Procedures Manual P510.
	
	
	A licensee must preserve all fuel and distance records for the period covered by the quarterly tax returns for any periods under audit in accordance with the laws of the base jurisdiction. IFTA Procedures Manual P510.
	
	
	The records maintained by an IFTA licensee must be adequate to enable the base jurisdiction to verify the distances traveled and fuel purchased by the licensee for the period under audit and to evaluate the accuracy of the licensee’s distance and fuel accounting systems for its fleet. IFTA Procedures Manual P530.
	
	
	If a licensee fails to maintain IFTA required records, the base jurisdiction may determine the tax liability based on the best information available using factors such as prior experience of the licensee, similar operations, industry averages, or other pertinent information. IFTA Art. XII, R1210.100.015, R1210.200.005; IFTA Audit Manual A350.200.
	
	
	The assessment made by a base jurisdiction under the procedure provided by IFTA is presumed to be correct and, in any case where the validity of the assessment is questioned, the burden is on the licensee to establish by a fair preponderance of evidence that the assessment is erroneous or excessive. IFTA Art. XII, R1210.300; IFTA Procedures Manual P520.
	
	
	Staff’s evidence supports a conclusion that the audit was based on the best information available and conducted pursuant to IFTA procedures; therefore, it is presumed correct.
	
	
	The ALJ finds that Petitioner failed to establish that the audit was erroneous, and the assessment should be upheld.
	
	
	An IFTA base jurisdiction may assess the licensee a penalty of $50.00 or 10 percent of delinquent taxes, whichever is greater, for failing to file a tax return, filing a late tax return, [or] underpaying taxes due. IFTA Art. R1220.100. Penalty may be waived “for reasonable cause.” IFTA Art. XII, R1260.100.
	
	
	Petitioner failed to provide evidence regarding a reasonable cause that led to the amount of tax due and, in light of the absence of any evidence, the ALJ concludes penalty should be upheld.
	
	
	The IFTA base jurisdiction, for itself and on behalf of the other jurisdictions, assesses interest on all delinquent taxes due to each jurisdiction except taxes collected directly by other jurisdictions. See IFTA Art. XII, R1230.
	
	
	If a licensee can demonstrate a tax return was filed late because of misinformation given to the carrier by the base jurisdiction, the interest may be waived for the base jurisdiction if the jurisdiction’s statutes allow such a waiver. IFTA Art. XII, R1260.
	
	
	Petitioner failed to provide documentation of the existence of any of the factors meriting waiver of interest.
	
	
	Petitioner’s request for interest waiver should be denied.
	


 

Signed November 4, 2025.

 

Kimberly LaFlair

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 



 

 


ENDNOTES



[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf



[2] At present, insufficient information is available to determine which items and amounts are disputed or undisputed for purposes of Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112. In the absence of this information, the Comptroller will assume the entire amount of the assessment, as it appears in Comptroller’s Decision Attachment A, the Notification of Hearing Results, remains in dispute.

If Petitioner intends to sue the comptroller to dispute an amount of tax, penalty, or interest assessed in a deficiency redetermination or jeopardy determination under Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 111, Petitioner is required to file a motion for rehearing that “states the specific grounds of error and the disputed amounts associated with the grounds of error.” Tex. Tax Code § 112.201(a)(3). Petitioner should refer to Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112, for further guidance regarding a suit after redetermination.



[3] See Tex. Tax Code § 111.0081(c). 

[4] Petitioner’s Exhibit List purported to contain Exhibits 1-11. However, there was no Exhibit 6 in any of the provided documentation. The ALJ therefore numbered the exhibits sequentially as Exhibits 1-10.
subjects: BUSINESS RECORDS INCOMPLETE/INADEQUATE - ESTIMATED ASSESSMENT BASED ON BEST INFORMATION AVAILABLE
